title: Timelock propose/execute with packed (done|readyTimestamp) storage and predecessor dependency check
trigger_patterns: operation state Unset Waiting Ready Done
explanation: Comprehensive integration of multiple optimizations: (1) Operation slot derivation uses 41-byte keccak input — id at offset 0x00 (32 bytes) followed by _TIMELOCK_SLOT at offset 0x09 (9 bytes truncated from 32). The mstore(0x09, _TIMELOCK_SLOT) writes 32 bytes but only the lower 9 are within the keccak range. This packs id+slot-seed into less than the standard 64-byte mapping derivation. (2) Operation state is packed as `(readyTimestamp << 1) | done` — bit 0 is the done flag, bits [1..255] hold the ready timestamp. propose stores `shl(1, r)` (done=0); execute marks done via `sstore(s, or(1, p))` preserving the timestamp. State derivation is branchless: `mul(iszero(iszero(p)), add(and(p,1), sub(2, lt(timestamp(), shr(1, p)))))` returns 0/1/2/3 for Unset/Waiting/Ready/Done. (3) Predecessor check uses calldataload(opData.offset) to read the first 32 bytes of opData as the predecessor id, then sload(keccak256(0x00, 0x29)) checks its done bit. The combined `or(iszero(b), and(1, sload(...)))` evaluates true if no predecessor (b=0) OR predecessor is done. (4) Re-check after _execute prevents reentrancy: a malicious target call could have called cancel() within the same tx, so we verify the operation is still Ready before marking it done.
when_to_apply: Governance timelocks, multi-step admin operations with execution delay, ERC7821-compatible secure execution flows
when_not_to_apply: Simple immediate-execution accounts, contexts where the readyTimestamp may exceed 2^254 (centuries in the future)
